upperAir: The KOAK sounding shows a very stable, nearly isothermal profile with only a -1.46°C/km lapse rate from surface to 850mb — far below dry adiabatic (9.8°C/km), indicating a strong low-level inversion capping convection well below 1500m. The 850mb level at ~1512m is actually warmer than the surface relative to a normal lapse rate, confirming a classic marine inversion. Winds are light and variable below 850mb (320° at 6 kts surface), veering to 210° at 850mb then 245° at 700mb, with modest shear but no significant upper-level energy. Thermal ceiling today is effectively capped near 400-550m AGL based on BL height data — adequate for local soaring but no overdevelopment risk.
aviationFlags: No active AIRMETs for the SF Bay Area. KHAF reporting mist/BR with 4-6SM visibility and VRB/W winds 6-8 kts — marine layer remnants lingering at the coast. KSFO had FEW008 at 1956Z (low marine stratus to ~800ft) clearing to BKN200 by 2056Z. KRHV clear and warm (27°C) with 280-310° winds 7-8 kts — clean inland valley conditions. KAPC 21°C, 240° 7 kts, clear. KWVI 17°C, 210-220° 8 kts, clear. Overall benign aviation environment inland; coastal mist at KHAF is the only notable anomaly.

sites.name: Ed Levin (1750 ft)
sites.locationDescriptor: Milpitas — inland hill, NW-facing launch, thermal/ridge hybrid
sites.howItWorks: Ed Levin's high launch at 1750ft works best when afternoon thermal cycles develop from valley heating, or when a moderate NW-NNW sea breeze provides ridge lift. Ideal wind is 8-15 kts from 310-350° at launch. Light and variable mornings are typical; the sea breeze front usually arrives by midday.
sites.todaySetup: Today is a MARGINAL but usable thermal day at Ed Levin 1750ft, with the limiting factor being light and erratic winds through late morning and a BL height that stays modest (400-555m). The morning is dead calm with southerly drainage flow transitioning around 10-11 AM to light variable/NW — not ideal for any launch. By noon the NW sea breeze fills in at 4.5-6.6 kts from 313-345°, which is on-heading but on the soft side for reliable ridge lift. BL height climbs to ~535-555m by 2-3 PM, giving modest but usable thermal depth for the site. CAPE is zero and no precip, so conditions are benign if light. The afternoon cloud cover spike (44-86% at 3-4 PM) is worth watching — likely high cirrus advection, not CU — and clears again by 5 PM. KRHV confirms warm, clear inland conditions (27°C, 280-310° 7-8 kts) supporting the thermal cycle. Expect light, punchy thermals rather than a solid organized climb.

hourlyWindows.label: Morning (8–11 AM)
hourlyWindows.summary: Light southerly drainage 1-3.5 kts shifting variable by 10 AM — BL only 115-345m. Not flyable; wait for sea breeze to establish.

hourlyWindows.label: Midday (11 AM–2 PM)
hourlyWindows.summary: NW sea breeze builds 4.5-5.8 kts from 313-345° with BL 405-535m. Best window of the day — light but on-heading; experienced P3 pilots can work light thermals.

hourlyWindows.label: Afternoon (2 PM–sunset)
hourlyWindows.summary: Wind holds 5.8-6.6 kts NNW through 5 PM, BL peaks near 555m then settles back to 470m by 6 PM. Flyable but light; cloud cover surges 44-86% mid-afternoon before clearing — monitor for any lift suppression.

verdict.hazards: Light and variable morning, modest BL cap ~550m, erratic cloud coverage 3-4 PM, zero CAPE but also zero organized lift

sites.name: Ed Levin (600 ft)
sites.locationDescriptor: Milpitas — mid-hill launch, W-facing, valley thermal site
sites.howItWorks: The 600ft launch at Ed Levin is a lower bowl launch ideal for training and lighter wind days. It benefits from valley thermals and gentle W-NW flow. Works best with 5-12 kts from 270-320°. It's more sheltered than the high launch but also more susceptible to cross or off-heading flow.
sites.todaySetup: The 600ft launch has the best alignment window of the Ed Levin launches today. Wind shifts from light southerly to W-NW by 10-11 AM (3.4-5.7 kts at 280-285°) — nearly on-heading for this W-facing site. This is the prime window before the flow backs more NNW at upper levels. The BL climbs to 280-440m by noon, sufficient for local soaring in the low bowl. Speeds remain in a comfortable 4-7 kt range through the afternoon, peaking at 6.8 kts from 323° at 5 PM. This site is accessible to P2 pilots during the benign midday hours when flow is organized. The afternoon cloud cover (25-49% at 2-4 PM) is not problematic. KRHV data confirms the supportive thermal environment.

hourlyWindows.label: Morning (8–11 AM)
hourlyWindows.summary: Light southerly 1-2 kts transitioning to W by 10 AM at 3.4 kts. Borderline — 11 AM brings the best early launch conditions at 5.7 kts from 280°.

hourlyWindows.label: Midday (11 AM–2 PM)
hourlyWindows.summary: Solid 5-6 kts from 280-307° W-NNW — best organized flow of the day. BL 280-575m gives room to work. Recommended window for P2+ pilots.

hourlyWindows.label: Afternoon (2 PM–sunset)
hourlyWindows.summary: Wind shifts slightly more NNW (318-346°) at 5-7 kts — still usable but slightly more cross for this W-facing launch. Flow organizes again by 5-6 PM at 6-7 kts NNW.

verdict.hazards: Light morning, direction shift to NNW afternoon, modest BL height, not ideal for extended soaring

sites.name: Ed Levin (300 ft)
sites.locationDescriptor: Milpitas — training hill, W-facing, beginner launch
sites.howItWorks: The 300ft training hill is purely a thermal/valley breeze site used for student and recent P2 flights. It requires consistent W-NW flow of 5-12 kts and a developing thermal cycle. Flights are low and short — the site is forgiving but needs at least moderate organized wind.
sites.todaySetup: Today's 300ft launch window is actually the strongest of the three Ed Levin launches in terms of surface wind speed, with W-NW flow from 6.2-7.8 kts between 11 AM and 1 PM from 283-291° — excellent heading alignment. The BL reaches 255-450m, more than adequate for student soaring flights. The afternoon holds 6.5-7.6 kts from 286-299°, keeping the site active through ~5 PM. The key limiter is the early morning light/southerly drainage flow making pre-10 AM launches unsuitable. Afternoon cloud build (31-45%) is not a concern. This is the most flyable of the three Ed Levin launches today due to its stronger and better-aligned surface flow.

hourlyWindows.label: Morning (8–11 AM)
hourlyWindows.summary: Light variable 1-4.5 kts with southerly component until ~10 AM — unsuitable for launch. Conditions improve quickly by 10-11 AM as W flow fills in.

hourlyWindows.label: Midday (11 AM–2 PM)
hourlyWindows.summary: Best window: 6-7.8 kts from 283-289° W — on-heading and organized. BL 350-435m. Good training conditions; P2 appropriate.

hourlyWindows.label: Afternoon (2 PM–sunset)
hourlyWindows.summary: Wind holds 6.5-7.6 kts from 286-299° through 5 PM before dropping to 3.3 kts at 7 PM. Extended usable afternoon window for the training hill.

verdict.hazards: Light variable morning, modest BL, afternoon cloud transients — all manageable

sites.name: Mussel Rock
sites.locationDescriptor: Daly City — coastal bluff, W-facing ridge, pure ridge lift site
sites.howItWorks: Mussel Rock is a classic W-facing coastal ridge site driven entirely by the Pacific onshore flow. It needs consistent W-WSW winds of 10-20 kts at ridge height for good ridge lift soaring. Too light and there's no lift; too strong and rotor risk increases below the bluff. Marine layer burn-off timing is critical — fog can keep the site socked in until midday.
sites.todaySetup: Mussel Rock is MARGINAL-to-NO FLY today due to winds on the light side for reliable ridge soaring. The site gets W-WSW flow at 5-8 kts surface (262-275°) which is on-heading but well below the 10-15 kt threshold needed for solid ridge lift at this bluff. The marine layer was active this morning — KHAF reporting mist/BR with 4-6SM visibility and VRB-W flow — indicating the coastal fog hasn't fully cleared. SST at SF Bar buoy is cold at 12.5°C (Half Moon Bay buoy slightly warmer at 14.5°C), which promotes a moderate marine layer that is slow to burn off. By noon conditions clear at the coast based on KHAF observations, but winds remain light (5-7 kts at model level). The 80m wind of 6.7-8.2 kts through the afternoon gives a hint of gradient aloft, but surface values are marginal. Afternoon cloud coverage at 88-99% from 4-5 PM (likely stratus return) further limits the usable window. Experienced ridge soaring pilots may find a workable window 12-3 PM but expect light, punchy, and inconsistent conditions.

hourlyWindows.label: Morning (8–11 AM)
hourlyWindows.summary: Marine layer mist and 100% overcast through 11 AM per model — coastal fog lingering. Winds 4-7 kts W-WSW but visibility compromised. Not recommended for launch.

hourlyWindows.label: Midday (11 AM–2 PM)
hourlyWindows.summary: Best window: overcast clears to 10-11% cloud cover, winds 5-7 kts from 262-280° W. On-heading but marginal strength — light ridge lift possible, not reliable soaring.

hourlyWindows.label: Afternoon (2 PM–sunset)
hourlyWindows.summary: Winds hold 5-7 kts WSW but evening stratus returns by 4-5 PM (88-99% cloud cover). Window closes as marine layer reinvades; conditions deteriorate by 4 PM.

verdict.hazards: Marine layer fog morning and evening, winds too light for solid ridge lift, rotor below bluff in light flow, cold SST 12.5°C supporting persistent marine layer

sites.name: Mission Peak
sites.locationDescriptor: Fremont — inland ridge, NW-facing, thermal/ridge hybrid
sites.howItWorks: Mission Peak is a NW-facing inland ridge site that works on a combination of NW sea breeze and thermal development from the warm Fremont valley. Ideal winds are 8-15 kts from 310-350°. The site rewards patient pilots who can time the sea breeze arrival and thermal trigger, and it can produce strong conditions later in the afternoon.
sites.todaySetup: Mission Peak conditions today closely mirror Ed Levin 1750ft — they share nearly identical model data, which makes sense given their proximity and similar elevation/aspect. The deciding factors are the same: light, variable early morning flow unsuitable for launch, transitioning to a NW sea breeze (4.5-6.6 kts from 313-345°) by noon. BL height climbs to 405-555m by 1-3 PM, giving some thermal depth. KRHV confirms the inland warmth (27°C, NW 7-8 kts) that drives the afternoon valley breeze. The afternoon cloud cover 44-86% at 3-4 PM is the same transient pattern as Ed Levin and clears. With CAPE at zero and zero precip, there's no convective risk. Mission Peak tends to be slightly more thermally active due to its south-facing slope heating but today's weak lapse rate caps that advantage. Conditions are light and mellow — suitable for experienced P2s and P3s who can work gentle thermals.

hourlyWindows.label: Morning (8–11 AM)
hourlyWindows.summary: Southerly drainage 1-3.5 kts transitioning to variable — BL only 115-345m. Not suitable for launch; wait for NW sea breeze to arrive.

hourlyWindows.label: Midday (11 AM–2 PM)
hourlyWindows.summary: NW sea breeze 4.5-5.8 kts from 313-345° with BL 405-535m — on-heading, usable. Best thermal window of the day; light but organized.

hourlyWindows.label: Afternoon (2 PM–sunset)
hourlyWindows.summary: Wind holds 6-6.6 kts NNW through 4 PM, BL peaks near 555m. Cloud cover 44-86% at 3-4 PM may briefly suppress thermals before clearing by 5 PM.

verdict.hazards: Light morning, low BL inversion cap, transient afternoon overcast 3-4 PM, light thermals only

tomorrow.name: Ed Levin (1750 ft)
tomorrow.analysis: Sunday March 29 looks stronger and more complex at the high launch. GFS/HRRR agree on 9-13 kts from 020-032° in the morning — a northerly component that is off-heading for this NW-facing site. Tomorrow.io adds gusts to 15-19 kts by midday. 100% cloud cover from noon onward per both models, with winds shifting back to 315-344° NW in the afternoon at 8-10 kts. The overcast and off-heading morning flow make this a challenging day; the NW afternoon shift could be usable for experienced pilots if clouds lift, but gust potential (Tomorrow.io shows 15-18 kt gusts) makes it P3+ territory. MARGINAL to NO FLY depending on cloud break.

tomorrow.name: Ed Levin (600 ft)
tomorrow.analysis: Strong NNE/N flow 10-14 kts morning is off-heading and too strong for the 600ft W-facing launch. Tomorrow.io gusts reach 12-15 kts. Overcast from noon. Similar story to 1750ft — morning is unsuitable, afternoon NW shift 7-10 kts could work but cloud cover suppresses thermals all day. MARGINAL at best, NO FLY likely.

tomorrow.name: Ed Levin (300 ft)
tomorrow.analysis: Morning NNE 5.5 kts is off-heading. Flow goes variable by midday with overcast. Afternoon NW 7-10 kts from 307-327° is better aligned but 100% cloud cover persists. Training hill flights possible in a narrow afternoon window if overcast thins, but not recommended. MARGINAL.

tomorrow.name: Mussel Rock
tomorrow.analysis: Very light winds Sunday — GFS/HRRR show 1.6-5.7 kts from variable directions, with Tomorrow.io confirming near-calm (1-2.3 kts). 100% overcast all day. This is a NO FLY day at Mussel Rock — no ridge lift possible in calm conditions and zero visibility prospects through the overcast.

tomorrow.name: Mission Peak
tomorrow.analysis: Same pattern as Ed Levin 1750ft — strong NNE/N 9-13 kts morning off-heading, then overcast all day, with afternoon NW shift 8-10 kts. Tomorrow.io gusts 11-15 kts. The strong morning flow and persistent overcast make Sunday a pass. MARGINAL to NO FLY.

day2.name: Ed Levin (1750 ft)
day2.analysis: Monday March 30: 100% overcast all day per GFS. Light NW flow 1-8 kts with precip probability up to 6% by evening. BL reaches 730m in the afternoon which is better, but the overcast kills thermal development. Not a flyable day — NO FLY.

day2.name: Ed Levin (600 ft)
day2.analysis: Monday: Same 100% overcast, light 1-8 kts NW flow, precip creeping up to 5% by evening. No thermal prospects under solid cloud cover. NO FLY.

day2.name: Ed Levin (300 ft)
day2.analysis: Monday: Identical cloud and wind picture to other Ed Levin launches — 100% overcast, light W-NW, precip risk increasing. NO FLY.

day2.name: Mussel Rock
day2.analysis: Monday: Solid overcast, W-WSW 5-8 kts with some afternoon pickup to 7.8 kts at 80m. Ridge lift technically possible if winds hold at surface, but 100% cloud cover all day and increasing precip risk (3-6%) make this unappealing. MARGINAL to NO FLY.

day2.name: Mission Peak
day2.analysis: Monday: 100% overcast, light variable flow shifting from NE morning to SW-W afternoon, with strong 80m winds 6-12 kts suggesting gradient aloft decoupled from surface. Increasing precip (2-5%). NO FLY.

day3.name: Ed Levin (1750 ft)
day3.analysis: Tuesday March 31: 100% overcast persists with CAPE jumping to 13-30% — elevated convective potential suggesting an unstable airmass arriving. Precip probability 13-30%. Light surface winds but increasing CAPE is a convective hazard flag. NO FLY — potential shower/thunderstorm risk.

day3.name: Ed Levin (600 ft)
day3.analysis: Tuesday: Same unstable pattern — CAPE 12-27%, precip 12-27%, 100% overcast. Convective risk increasing. NO FLY.

day3.name: Ed Levin (300 ft)
day3.analysis: Tuesday: CAPE 12-25%, precip 12-25%, overcast all day. Convective instability with light surface winds is a dangerous combination — unpredictable conditions. NO FLY.

day3.name: Mussel Rock
day3.analysis: Tuesday: W-WSW 5-10 kts with afternoon pickup to 9-10 kts at 240° — a more southerly component. CAPE 17-28%, precip 17-28%, 100% overcast. Possible shower activity. Ridge winds may be flyable speed-wise but the convective environment makes this dangerous. NO FLY.

day3.name: Mission Peak
day3.analysis: Tuesday: CAPE 13-28%, precip 13-28%, overcast all day. Light variable surface flow. The increasing CAPE and precip probability signal a convective weather system moving through. NO FLY.

watchlist: Marine layer burn-off at Mussel Rock — KHAF mist/BR and cold SST (12.5°C SF Bar) suggest coastal fog could linger past noon; check KHAF METAR updates before driving out
Afternoon cloud surge 3-4 PM at Ed Levin all launches (44-86% coverage) — likely cirrus advection ahead of approaching system, not CU, but monitor for any lift suppression
Tomorrow.io flags gusts to 15-19 kts Sunday at Ed Levin — verify with morning HRRR update before flying Sunday
CAPE rising to 13-30% by Tuesday March 31 across all sites — approaching convective weather system; avoid flying Tuesday and monitor for earlier arrival
Precip probability creeps from 1% Sunday to 30% Tuesday — multi-day flyable window ends after today; Saturday is the pick of the week
tldr: Saturday is the best — and likely only — flyable day this stretch. Ed Levin 300ft is the standout with a solid 11 AM–4 PM window of 6-8 kts W-WNW on-heading flow; the high launch and Mission Peak are lighter and marginal but workable noon–3 PM for P3 pilots. Mussel Rock is technically on-heading but too light for reliable ridge soaring and the coastal fog is slow to clear. Everything looks worse Sunday through Tuesday as a system approaches with overcast, increasing precip, and CAPE building — get your flying in today.
